title: The Twenty Second Sunday after Pentecost - The Wilderness
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
October 22, 2018

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Twenty-Second Sunday after Pentecost in Ordinary Time, falling in the latter portion of the liturgical year before Advent begins on December 2, 2018. The RCL readings for this Sunday would have included Job 38:1-7 (God's response from the whirlwind), Psalm 104:1-9, 25, 37c, Hebrews 5:1-10, and Mark 10:35-45—passages focused on divine mystery, Christ's suffering, and servant leadership. The sermon title "The Wilderness" suggests the preacher engaged with the wilderness/desert imagery prominent in these texts and the liturgical season's contemplative character.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the immediate aftermath of its 79th General Convention (June 2018 in Austin, Texas), which had passed resolutions supporting LGBTQ+ inclusion and affirming various progressive stances. The Diocese of Colorado was under the leadership of Bishop Robert Wright (appointed 2017), who has been known for progressive theology. No major diocesan crises or bishop elections were occurring in October 2018, though the broader Anglican Communion remained fractured over sexuality and authority questions.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing unseasonably warm October weather, with temperatures in the 70s-80s Fahrenheit during the preceding week—atypical for late October. The city was focused on the November 2018 midterm elections, with Colorado races (Senate race between Democrat John Hickenlooper and Republican Walker Stapleton, congressional races) dominating local political coverage. Saint John's Cathedral, located in downtown Denver near Civic Center, was part of a neighborhood experiencing gentrification and increased homelessness visibility—issues that would have been evident to the congregation.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The midterm elections were the dominant story, occurring exactly two weeks away (November 6, 2018), with intense campaigning and early voting underway. The Kavanaugh Supreme Court confirmation hearings aftermath was still resonating; Justice Kavanaugh had been confirmed October 6, 2018, amid deep partisan divisions. The Trump administration was facing investigations, with the Mueller investigation ongoing and questions swirling about potential obstruction of justice; Michael Cohen's guilty plea (August 2018) had recently revealed campaign finance violations related to hush money payments.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Saudi journalist Jamal Khashoggi's disappearance (October 2, 2018) from the Saudi consulate in Istanbul was a major ongoing story with grave implications—his murder was becoming evident though not yet officially acknowledged, creating international outrage. The migrant caravan from Central America was making headlines as it moved north toward the U.S. border, becoming a flashpoint in the midterm election discourse and Trump administration rhetoric. The conflict in Yemen was continuing with ongoing Saudi-led airstrikes against Houthi forces, with humanitarian crisis reports emerging.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

America was deeply polarized and anxious in October 2018—midterm elections had become proxy warfare between opposing ideologies, with heightened rhetoric and social division visible everywhere. The #MeToo movement continued reshaping cultural conversations about power and accountability (Kavanaugh hearings being the latest flashpoint). In Denver specifically, there was a quiet undercurrent of anxiety about homelessness, economic inequality, and rapid change; nationally, the cultural mood was one of pre-election tension mixed with pandemic-free normalcy that few realized would end within 18 months.
